findings.title: Normalizing in the hook (state layer) is the correct boundary
findings.description: The design places normalization in useTerminalSplits (the single owner of TerminalSplitConfig state) rather than in TerminalSplitContainer (view). This is the right call: the view is one of potentially several consumers, the bad state is also persisted to localStorage, and only the state layer can self-heal the persisted value. Normalizing at render time would leave the invariant (sum=1.0) violated in state and storage and would duplicate the logic across consumers. This is SRP-aligned and matches the rejected-alternatives table in section 4.
findings.recommendation: No change. Keep normalization in the hook as designed.

findings.title: normalizeWidths is cohesive, pure, and directly unit-testable
findings.description: normalizeWidths(widths: number[]): number[] is a length-preserving pure function with a single responsibility (rescale to sum=1.0, equal-split fallback for sum<=0). It does not mutate input and returns a fresh array, consistent with the immutable update style used throughout the hook. The length-preserving fallback (1/widths.length) correctly preserves the widths.length === splits.length invariant, which a fixed [1] fallback would break for multi-element arrays.
findings.recommendation: Consider exporting normalizeWidths (or a thin re-export) so it can be unit-tested directly in addition to via the hook, but testing through removeSplit/readInitialState is acceptable. Not required.

findings.title: normalizeWidths fits existing module-scope helper conventions
findings.description: The hook already places pure helpers (cloneDefault, pickUnusedCliTool, widthsValid) at module scope above the hook body. normalizeWidths at the same scope is consistent in placement, naming (verbNoun, like cloneDefault), and immutability (returns new array like cloneDefault and the slice-based updates in addSplit/removeSplit). readInitialState returning `{ ...parsed, widths: normalizeWidths(parsed.widths) }` is consistent with the existing spread-based immutable updates (e.g. setSplitCliTool, setSplitWidth) and preserves the TerminalSplitConfig shape exactly (splits + widths), since only widths is replaced and splits is carried through via spread.

findings.title: useCallback dependency arrays remain correct ([] for removeSplit)
findings.description: removeSplit uses the functional setConfig(prev => ...) form and references only module-scope constants (MIN_SPLITS) and the module-scope normalizeWidths, so the empty dependency array stays valid. No new reactive dependency is introduced. readInitialState is not a hook callback and is unaffected by deps rules.

findings.title: Existing 'restores a valid stored config' test stays green under no-op normalization
findings.description: The existing test asserts widths toEqual([0.6, 0.4]). For a valid stored config sum is exactly 1.0, and 0.6/1.0===0.6 and 0.4/1.0===0.4 in IEEE754 (verified), so normalization is a true no-op for already-valid configs and the strict toEqual passes. No regression.
findings.recommendation: No change. Confirmed no regression to the existing load test.

findings.title: Acceptance criterion 'widths.reduce(sum) === 1.0' is floating-point-brittle; tests should use toBeCloseTo
findings.description: The design's test strategy item 1 and the issue acceptance criteria state the post-removeSplit sum must equal 1.0. After normalization, the sum of divided floats is NOT guaranteed to be exactly 1.0 in IEEE754. Verified: the specific examples chosen in the design ([0.5,0.5]->[0.5]->[1], and [0.6,0.3,0.1]->drop->[0.6/0.9,0.3/0.9]) happen to sum to exactly 1.0, but a general ratio such as [0.123,0.456] normalizes to a sum of 1.0000000000000002 (!== 1.0). If a regression test is written as `expect(sum).toBe(1.0)` / `=== 1.0` with arbitrary ratios it can fail spuriously. The runtime code is correct; only the test assertion wording is brittle.
findings.recommendation: Write the sum assertion as `expect(sum).toBeCloseTo(1, 10)` (or `toBeCloseTo(1)`), not `toBe(1.0)`/`=== 1.0`. Keep the chosen test fixtures, but make the assertion tolerance-based so future fixture changes don't introduce flakiness. Update the AC wording from 'sum === 1.0' to 'sum approximately 1.0 (toBeCloseTo)'.

findings.title: Persist useEffect write-back of normalized value is stable; no render loop
findings.description: On load, readInitialState returns the normalized config once as the initial useState value. The persist useEffect ([config, worktreeId]) then writes that normalized config to localStorage. Because normalization happens inside the initial state factory (not in an effect that calls setConfig), there is no setConfig triggered by the effect, hence no additional render/loop. removeSplit calls setConfig once per click; the persist effect fires once afterward. No oscillation: re-normalizing an already-normalized array is a near-idempotent no-op and, crucially, nothing reads localStorage again to feed setConfig within the same mount.
findings.recommendation: No change. The write-back is a one-shot self-heal, stable by construction.

findings.title: Legacy single-element [0.5] state is correctly healed at both removeSplit and load
findings.description: removeSplit producing a single remaining element [0.5] -> normalizeWidths([0.5]) = [0.5/0.5] = [1] (verified). On load, a persisted [0.5] passes isValidSplitConfig (each element > 0) and is then normalized to [1]. Both the live-action path and the cold-load path converge to sum=1.0, satisfying the self-recovery goal.
findings.recommendation: No change. Add the load-recovery case to the regression tests as the design already specifies (test strategy item 3).

findings.title: No existing test asserts exact post-removeSplit widths
findings.description: Confirmed by inspection of tests/unit/hooks/useTerminalSplits.test.ts: removeSplit is exercised only for splits.length (lines 153-167), focusedSplitIndex clamping (228-238), and CLI selection preservation (240-259). No test inspects widths values after removeSplit, so changing removeSplit to normalize introduces no regression. TerminalSplitContainer/Pane tests assert pane count/role/cursor, not widths values.
findings.recommendation: No change. The impact-scope claim in the design (section 6 / S3-001) is accurate.

findings.title: normalizeWidths cannot produce NaN/Infinity/negative on the load path and is throw-free
findings.description: On the load path, normalizeWidths runs only after isValidSplitConfig passes, which guarantees every element is a finite number > 0. Therefore sum > 0 (sum of positives), division is well-defined and yields finite positives summing to ~1.0; the sum<=0 fallback branch is unreachable there (defensive only). On the removeSplit path, prev.widths is a previously-validated, all-positive array; slicing off the last positive element leaves a non-empty all-positive array (MIN_SPLITS guard ensures length>=1 remains), so sum>0 again. The fallback 1/widths.length is finite for length>=1. The function performs only O(n) arithmetic (n<=3), allocates one array, never throws, and has no recursion/unbounded loops — no DoS or exception risk.
findings.recommendation: No change. Robustness is adequate. (Optional defensive note: if a future caller ever passed an empty array, 1/0 would yield Infinity; current callers never do, and isValidSplitConfig/MIN_SPLITS prevent it, so no guard is required.)

findings.title: No new external attack surface; input is self-origin localStorage only
findings.description: The only data flowing into normalizeWidths is the worktree-scoped localStorage payload that the app itself writes, already gated by JSON.parse-in-try/catch and isValidSplitConfig. No PII, no network input, no command/path/shell execution touched. The change is confined to numeric array math in the client state layer.

floating_point_verdict: The 'sum === 1.0' acceptance criterion is brittle and SHOULD use toBeCloseTo. The runtime normalization is correct, but normalized float sums are not guaranteed exactly 1.0 (verified counterexample [0.123,0.456] -> sum 1.0000000000000002). The design's chosen fixtures happen to land on exactly 1.0, but the assertion should be tolerance-based (toBeCloseTo(1)) to avoid future flakiness.
overall_assessment: The design is sound, minimal, and correctly scoped. Normalizing in the state layer (hook) rather than the view is the right architectural choice (SRP, single source of truth, enables localStorage self-heal). normalizeWidths is a cohesive, pure, immutable, throw-free helper consistent with existing module-scope helpers (cloneDefault/widthsValid). useCallback deps, TerminalSplitConfig shape preservation, and the no-op-on-valid-config behavior are all correct, and no existing test asserts exact post-removeSplit widths so there is no regression. Robustness is adequate: on both code paths the input is pre-validated all-positive, so no NaN/Infinity/negative/throw/DoS is possible. The single actionable item (Should Fix) is purely about test/AC wording: assert the post-normalization sum with toBeCloseTo(1) rather than === 1.0. Approve to implement with that test-assertion adjustment.
